The silent empty DependencyList returned on CheckConstraints failure is appropriate since a genuinely-failing instantiation throws ArgumentException from MakeGenericType at runtime anyway, and the 'wildcard now, runtime re-validates' direction is the right failure asymmetry.

IsCanonicalCastTarget's true-returning branches (__UniversalCanon match, __Canon+IsGCPointer true) are never exercised by any test; deleting the guard at CastingHelper.cs:176-179 would leave the entire suite green.
Branches C (type==__UniversalCanon) and D (type==__Canon) of IsCanonicalTypeArgMatch are never covered because every test puts the canonical type on the constraint side, never the param side.
The || IsCanonicalDefinitionType(type, Any) sub-clause of branch B (canon-vs-canon matching, e.g. __UniversalCanon vs __Canon) is not covered by any test.
The Category-mismatch negative case (SzArray vs MdArray) in the ParameterizedType recursion is not covered by any test.
The array Rank check is only trivially covered (SzArray rank 1==1); a genuine rank mismatch like string[,] vs __Canon[,,] is untested, and deleting the Rank check would break nothing in the suite.

The thisInst[i]==otherInst[i]->continue mixed-instantiation path in IsCanonEquivalent is not covered by any test -- no test compares e.g. Foo<int,string> vs Foo<int,__Canon>, precisely the denormalized shape the PR's own HandleCallAction comment calls out.
The ReferenceTypeConstraint<__Canon> assertion passes even without this PR because the caller's IsGCPointer check short-circuits first -- it pins behavior but does not cover new code.

The NotNullableValueTypeConstraint true arm for __UniversalCanon is dead by construction -- __UniversalCanon's IsValueType short-circuits before this arm runs, so the corresponding test exercises pre-existing code, not the new arm.

CanCastToConstraintWithCanon has all live branches covered including a subtle check-ordering regression guard (struct under __UniversalCanon constraint) that verifies the wildcard check runs before the value-type early-out.

The self IsCanonEquivalent check at CanCastToNonVariantInterface:426 is not covered by any test -- needs T to itself be the non-variant interface, but the only interface-as-param test uses the variant IGen instead.
The negative side of the variance-path integration point -- IsCanonicalTypeArgMatch returning false and control falling into the variance switch with a canon target arg -- is not covered by any test.

MakeGenericTypeSite is end-to-end covered by TestMakeGenericConstrainedDataflow, a faithful minimal repro of #126604 exercising NormalizeInstantiation, wildcard constraint checking, and real Activator.CreateInstance.
MakeGenericMethodSite has no smoke test, no unit test, and no NormalizeInstantiation call; the denormalized-shape half of the fix is asymmetric with the type site and completely unverified.

NonCanon stubs return constant false, making compiled behavior line-for-line identical to pre-PR (verified by reading the diff, not test); no new ILVerification test was added, acceptable given the structural equivalence.

System.Private.TypeLoader.csproj now compiles the Canon implementation into the runtime type loader, giving runtime CanCastTo (e.g. GVM dispatch resolution) canonical wildcard semantics with zero direct tests in the PR.

Comment says '__Canon matches object in invariant arg position of IGen' but IGen<in T> is contravariant; the match happens via the variance-agnostic short-circuit before the variance switch.

Block titled 'Parameterized canonical types (e.g., __Canon[] as type arg in constraint)' never constructs __Canon[]; its single assert re-exercises the already-covered wildcard-param branch -- the real parameterized coverage is elsewhere.

The test is one monolithic [Fact] with ~30 unlabeled asserts, which hides which assertion fails first, but this matches the file's established convention; assertion messages would still be a cheap improvement.
All assertions go through the public CheckConstraints/CanCastTo contract with no reflection on internals, so the tests are not brittle and will survive refactoring of the private helpers.
Negative assertions accompany most positive test blocks, guarding against the primary risk of this PR -- wildcard semantics silently over-accepting and suppressing legitimate constraint failures.

The struct-under-__UniversalCanon-constraint test case implicitly regression-guards the ordering of CanCastToConstraintWithCanon before the value-type early-out -- a subtle interaction that would break if the checks were reordered.

The smoke test is a faithful, minimal end-to-end repro of #126604: struct+class instantiation forcing a shared body, a mixed concrete/canonical MakeGenericType result exercising NormalizeInstantiation, and a real Activator.CreateInstance verifying dependencies were rooted.
The partial-class Canon/NonCanon split keeps ILVerification behavior provably unchanged by construction via constant-false stubs, which is stronger than test coverage for that consumer.

Test comment declares '__Canon matches object in invariant arg position of IGen' immediately after correctly noting IGen<in T> is contravariant -- self-contradictory; actual mechanism is the canon-arg check preceding variance consideration.

Test comment claims canonicalizing ComplexGenericConstraint3<IGen<int[]>, int[]> yields <__Canon, int[]> ('U=int[] stays'), but arrays canonicalize to __Canon too, so the true result would be <__Canon, __Canon>; the tested shape is actually a denormalized shape.

IsCanonicalCastTarget's doc says pointers/byrefs/function pointers are not valid instantiation arguments, but the method doesn't enforce this itself -- the Universal branch accepts them unconditionally, and enforcement isn't uniform across validators.

IsCanonicalTypeArgMatch's doc doesn't explain that the CanonicalFormKind.Any clause is load-bearing: without it, the (__UniversalCanon, __Canon) pairing would silently break the documented 'UniversalCanon matches any type' symmetry.
Minor: the doc's 'non-leaf types' wording is vague, and the Arg2 example type name used in the product-code comment is a test-assembly type -- comment-rot risk if the test assembly changes.

IsCanonEquivalent's doc implies same-definition non-generic pairs are equivalent, but the Length==0 early return always makes non-generic pairs return false; this undocumented contract only works because every call site pairs it with IsEquivalentTo.

CanCastToConstraintWithCanon's doc reads as if it's only called under a precondition, but the call site invokes it unconditionally; a false return doesn't mean the cast fails -- it means 'no wildcard rule applies, fall back to the regular check'.

The inline comment 'any concrete type substituted at runtime will be validated then' is a runtime-behavior claim not verifiable from this diff alone, stated as fact without a pointer to where that validation happens.

IsSpecialTypeMeetingConstraint has zero comments explaining why __Canon satisfies class/new() but not struct while __UniversalCanon satisfies all three, unlike the neighboring CheckGenericSpecialConstraint which has an explanatory comment.
Test comment 'constraint has canonical subtype' at line 439 uses 'subtype' ambiguously, colliding with the term of art IsCanonicalSubtype.
Test comment ':440 Arg2<string> canonicalizes to Arg2<__Canon>' is imprecise -- the actual mechanism exercised is IsCanonEquivalent structural matching, not canonicalization.

The canon short-circuit inside the variance loop (CastingHelper.cs:477-478) -- making a canon match satisfy any variance position -- is the most behaviorally significant insertion in the PR and has no comment.
IsCanonicalCastTarget is checked before the category switch in CanCastToInternal -- the ordering that makes __UniversalCanon accept pointer/byref/fnptr categories -- with no comment explaining it.

The Canon/NonCanon partial-class linking scheme is entirely undocumented -- the stub files give no explanation that each consuming project must compile exactly one counterpart, and these are the only two .NonCanon.cs files in the tree.
MakeGenericMethodSite doesn't normalize while its sibling MakeGenericTypeSite does, with a comment explaining only the type case -- nothing records whether method sites are exempt by design.
No comment in the PR merely restates code (no removals recommended); passing nits noted: unused using System.Diagnostics and 8-space indentation inconsistency in ILVerification.projitems.

The HandleCallAction.cs:787 comment explaining the denormalized-shape risk is accurate, matches NormalizeInstantiation's semantics exactly, and downstream code demonstrably expects normalized shapes -- a model 'why' comment.
Aside from the two flagged inaccuracies, the test comment blocks were verified fully accurate against CoreTestAssembly/GenericConstraints.cs across several traced scenarios.
The two inline comments in IsCanonicalTypeArgMatch (lines 46-47, 51-52) accurately describe the code paths they annotate.
Verified consumer matrix confirms exactly three projects face the Canon/NonCanon choice; ten further downstream projects consume ILCompiler.TypeSystem as an assembly reference and never face it.

The 'exactly one variant included' invariant is compile-enforced (CS0103 if none, CS0111 if both) but expressed nowhere -- no declaration in the base file, no header comment in any variant file, no README.

Choosing the Canon variant transitively requires the whole Canon file family; for ILVerification the NonCanon choice is forced by compilation, not merely policy, and that coupling is invisible until you hit the errors.

The new pattern is a coherent extension of the existing Canon partial-file family idiom, where partial files add members that non-canon Common code never calls -- this PR is the first time Common code calls into canon-conditional members.

CastingHelper already has a classic partial-method hook that elides silently when unimplemented; the PR could have reused that shape but instead introduced a second, different optional-behavior idiom in the same class.

An #if SUPPORTS_CANON approach would have a worse failure mode than the chosen file-splitting: a consumer forgetting the define would silently compile the non-canon path rather than fail loudly at compile time.

A virtual-method-on-TypeSystemContext alternative isn't viable without breaking layering and would convert a per-distribution build-time capability into per-instance runtime dispatch on hot casting paths.

LangVersion=preview permits C# 9 extended partial methods, which would have made the contract visible in the base file and turned a missing-variant error into a self-explanatory CS8795 -- a near-zero-cost improvement the PR missed.

IsCanonicalCastTarget correctly belongs on CastingHelper rather than TypeSystemContext, since the context owns canonical identity while these methods encode canonical cast/match semantics belonging with the casting lattice.

The kernel rule '__Canon = any ref type; __UniversalCanon = any type' is re-encoded as an if-pair four times across CastingHelper.Canon.cs and TypeSystemConstraintsHelpers.Canon.cs rather than centralized in one named predicate.

IsCanonEquivalent's arity precondition is self-protected internally but undocumented as an explicit assert, unlike the sibling CanCastByVarianceToInterfaceOrDelegate which asserts the equivalent condition explicitly.
The Length==0->false behavior means the IsCanonEquivalent+IsEquivalentTo pairing invariant lives purely in call-site convention rather than being expressed by name or doc.
IsCanonicalTypeArgMatch's name correctly signals symmetric semantics, but its doc under-describes the method since it also handles the neither-is-canonical recursive case.

IsCanonicalCastTarget's doc/code mismatch means its invariant (pointers/byrefs/fnptrs never valid instantiation args) exists only as an ambiguous comment, not an enforced check within the method itself.

The Canon variant of IsSpecialTypeMeetingConstraint throws UnreachableException on invalid input while the NonCanon stub returns false for the same invalid input -- the two implementations of one logical contract disagree.

The NormalizeInstantiation call in HandleCallAction.cs restores the 'no denormalized shapes flow into CheckConstraints' invariant, which is maintained purely by call-site convention -- a pre-existing design weakness the PR conforms to.

NonVariantGenImpl<T> mildly diverges from the file's positional ArgN implementer naming convention in favor of a clearer name -- defensible but inconsistent.

using System.Diagnostics; is unused in the NonCanon stub file (only needed by the Canon counterpart for UnreachableException).

Security analysis traced three soundness questions to runtime evidence and found no secrets, injection, deserialization, or supply-chain surface — the ILVerify relaxed path is compiled out, MakeGenericType validates concrete args at runtime, and TypeLoader's runtime change affects only metadata-level GVM resolution, not memory-safety-critical object casts.
The Canon/NonCanon variant split is applied idiomatically, matches the 18 existing *.Canon.cs files, and the NonCanon no-op stubs cleanly preserve prior behavior for ILVerification.

TestCanonicalTypeConstraints is unusually thorough for the intended path — consistent positive/negative pairing gives real confidence the checks aren't vacuously true, and both IsCanonEquivalent integration points in CanCastToNonVariantInterface are genuinely exercised.
Within the intended dataflow use, the change is monotonic (adds only true-returning paths), erring toward over-rooting (the safe direction) rather than under-rooting.
